mentions.author: Surfinite
mentions.content:
## xYotsu vs Hey — `uP8mG-tr75d`
**Ranked Arena | 60+60/60 | 39 turns | xYotsu (P1) wins**

**Set:** Shadowfang, Doomed Mech, Vivid Drone, Innervi Field, Drake, Apollo, Mega Drone, Galvani Drone

This set screams **blue**. Drake (12BB, passive 2 atk + sac Blastforge for 2 more), Apollo (13BBB, snipe anything ≤3HP), and Doomed Mech (9BB, prompt 5HP blocker + 2 atk) all demand massive Blastforge infrastructure. The question isn't *whether* you go blue — it's how greedily you can ramp into it.

### The Opening (T1-10): Drone City

Both players read the set identically and go for an extremely greedy econ ramp. The first 8 turns are pure economy — Drones, Engineers, Blastforges. By T9-10, both players have committed to **triple Blastforge**. Hey actually goes to *four* on T10. Clear signal: both are racing to Apollo.

Notably absent is any early aggression. No Tarsiers, no Steelsplitters, no Rhinos. On a set where Apollo snipes low-HP units and the endgame is Doomed Mech rotation, early chip damage is just throwing resources away.

window.author: Surfinite
window.content:
### The Commit (T11-14): Apollo Mirror

**Turn 11 is the hinge.** xYotsu drops **Apollo**. Its snipe (destroy any unit ≤3HP) is devastating here — picks off Galvani Drones (1HP, undefendable), Engineers (1HP), and crucially **Innervi Fields** (3HP). It demands an immediate response.

Hey answers T12 with their own Apollo + Gauss Cannon. The mirror is locked in.

Then the arms race: xYotsu gets Drake + Steelsplitter + Wall (T13), Hey responds with Drake + Wall + Innervi Field + Forcefield (T14). Hey's early Innervi Field is forward-thinking — cheap green defense that regenerates, buying time for the blue army to come online.

### The Escalation (T15-20): Drake Stacking and Mega Drone Innovation

The middle game explodes. xYotsu gets a **second Drake** on T15. Hey fires back T16 with the biggest buy of the game: **Drake + double Doomed Mech + Blastforge + Innervi Field + 3 Drones + Engineer** — nine purchases in one turn.

Then T17 — xYotsu unveils the tech that arguably wins this game: **Mega Drone**. At a cost of 5g + 3H + 4 attack, Mega Drone converts offensive pressure into 4 gold/turn of sustained economy. xYotsu eventually buys **three** of these vs Hey's one. That's 12g/turn vs 4g/turn of converted economy — a structural advantage that compounds every single turn.

window.author: Surfinite
window.content:
### The Grind (T21-35): Attrition Warfare

Both players settle into a rhythm: Doomed Mech (prompt defense + 2 atk), Steelsplitter/Gauss Cannon (permanent attack), Innervi Field (cheap prompt defense), top up with Galvani Drones. Repeat. Every. Single. Turn.

xYotsu buys **11 Doomed Mechs** total, Hey buys **10**. With lifespan 5, these expire constantly and must be replaced. The player who sustains the rotation longer wins.

**But the clock tells a different story.** xYotsu is under crushing time pressure — multiple turns at exactly 0.0s bank. Turn 27 is a war crime against the clock: **90 clicks, 12 undos, 60.2s spent**. Meanwhile Hey plays comfortably with 5-20s of bank.

And yet xYotsu is *winning*. The Mega Drone economy edge means slightly more buys every turn. Steelsplitters diverge: xYotsu accumulates **7** to Hey's **2**, backed by 7 Gauss Cannons to Hey's 6. More persistent attackers = more pressure = Hey spends more on defense = less for counterattack. The slow squeeze is on.

window.author: Surfinite
window.content:
### The Kill (T36-39): The Position Collapses

T36: Hey makes a desperate play — **third Drake** + Wall + double Innervi Field + Forcefield. Drake sacs a Blastforge for burst attack. One big push.

T37: xYotsu absorbs it with heavy defense — Wall, Doomed Mech, Conduit, double Engineer, Innervi Field, Forcefield. Seven purchases. The punch lands on a brick wall.

T38 reveals Hey's position is crumbling: just **Innervi Field + Wall + Forcefield** bought despite 65 clicks of frantic unit management. The Drakes have eaten the Blastforges, the gold goes to replacement blockers, and there's nothing left for counterattack.

T39: xYotsu's final buy — Doomed Mech, Innervi Field, Gauss Cannon, Drone — and that's enough. Hey's defenses can't hold.

### What Decided This Game?

**Mega Drone. Three of them.** Near-identical openings, identical Apollo timings, similar Drake investment — the difference was sustained economy. xYotsu's 3 Mega Drones produced 12 extra gold/turn vs Hey's 4g/turn. Over a 20-turn grind, that's ~160 gold of extra purchasing power.

Hey's better time management wasn't enough. Sometimes in Prismata, the player scrambling at 0.0s with 12 undos per turn is the one winning on the board.

*What if Hey had matched Mega Drone production earlier?* Hey's second Mega Drone didn't come until T20, when xYotsu already had 2. If Hey had prioritized conversion on T18-19, the grind might have played out very differently.

39 turns. 11 Doomed Mechs each. An Apollo mirror. Decided by who converted their economy better. A proper slugfest.

window.author: Surfinite
window.content:
How the AI commentary bot handled being wrong:

Wonderboat quoted "Mega Drone. Three of them xD" — bot interpreted "xD" as laughing with the commentary. Said "the punchline landed."

I asked "What did you mean by Three of them?" — instead of questioning itself, it doubled down and confidently explained a 3 vs 1 Mega Drone economy thesis with specific numbers (12g/turn vs 4g/turn, ~160 gold advantage over 20 turns).

I quoted the line back at it — it still didn't catch on. Thought I was pointing out awkward grammar and offered to reword it.

I told it directly "You can only ever buy one Mega Drone each?" — only then did it realise Mega Drone is legendary supply 1, and its click parser was counting failed clicks and undo re-clicks as actual purchases. The entire core thesis was fabricated from bad data.

Wonderboat was laughing at the error, not at a clever punchline. Three chances to catch it, defended the wrong answer each time.

The commentary bot needs a supply-awareness patch before it goes live.

### The Opening (T1-10): Greedy Mirror

window.author: Surfinite
window.content:
### The Commit (T11-16): Apollo Mirror into Drake Arms Race

**T11 is the hinge.** xYotsu drops **Apollo** — snipe anything ≤3HP. On this board that means Galvani Drones, Engineers, and Innervi Fields are all targets. Hey answers T12 with their own Apollo + Gauss Cannon.

Then both escalate fast: xYotsu gets Drake T13, Hey matches T14 with Drake + Innervi Field + Forcefield. By T15-16 it's fully committed — xYotsu picks up a second Drake, Hey responds with **two Drakes in one turn** plus double Doomed Mech. Both players now have Apollo + 2 Drakes and a wall of Doomed Mechs. The arms race is locked in.

T17 brings the last key tech: both players pick up **Mega Drone** (xYotsu T17, Hey T20), converting excess attack into 4g/turn of sustained economy. With one each, the economy conversion is even — neither player gets an edge here.

window.author: Surfinite
window.content:
### The Grind (T21-35): Doomed Mech Rotation

This is where the game becomes a war of attrition. Both players settle into a rhythm: Doomed Mech (prompt 5HP + 2 atk, lifespan 5 — needs constant replacement), Steelsplitter/Gauss Cannon (permanent attack), Innervi Field (cheap prompt green defense). Repeat for 15 turns.

**The clock diverges.** xYotsu is under crushing time pressure — 0.0s bank on turns 21, 23, 25, 27, 29, 33, 39. Turn 27 is brutal: **90 clicks, 60.2 seconds, bank at 0.2s**. Meanwhile Hey stays relatively comfortable at 5-20s bank.

But the purchasing patterns diverge too. xYotsu leans into persistent attack — accumulating Steelsplitters and Gauss Cannons turn after turn. Hey leans defensive — more Walls, more Innervi Fields, more Forcefields. In a war of attrition, the player who maintains offensive output while defending efficiently is the one applying pressure. The one forced to over-invest in defense is slowly falling behind.

window.author: Surfinite
window.content:
### The Kill (T36-40): Position Collapse

T36: Hey plays the third Drake — a desperation move. Drake sacs a Blastforge for burst attack, but that Blastforge isn't coming back. Short-term power at the cost of long-term blue production.

T37-38 tell the story. xYotsu buys 7 units (Wall, Doomed Mech, Conduit, 2 Engineers, Innervi Field, Forcefield). Hey manages just **3 units** in 65 clicks — Innervi Field, Wall, Forcefield. The economy is gone. All those Blastforges sacrificed to Drake abilities, all those resources spent on Walls and Forcefields — there's nothing left to buy attackers with.

T40: Hey's final turn. Wall, Innervi Field. Two purchases. That's the sound of a position crumbling.

### What Decided This Game?

Not one single tech choice — both players built virtually identical armies (Apollo, 2+ Drakes, Mega Drone, Doomed Mech rotation). The ~200 rating point gap showed up in **efficiency through the grind**. xYotsu consistently bought more persistent attackers while Hey over-invested in defense. Over 15+ turns of attrition, that imbalance compounded — Hey needed more defense because xYotsu had more attack, which left less for Hey's own attack, which meant less pressure on xYotsu, which meant xYotsu could invest even more in attack.

Hey's third Drake (T36) was the final symptom, not the cause. By that point, the squeeze was already fatal.

40 turns of Doomed Mech rotation, an Apollo mirror, and a 200-rating-point gap that expressed itself one Steelsplitter at a time. Slow strangulation.

window.author: SilentSlayers
window.content:
Fun set indeed. I win a lot of these cluster zem sets. A big thing people get wrong is to get too little conduits and to wait too long to cluster. It's ok to cluster with no permanent damage behind it before zemora comes online. It just needs to deflate your opponent to be worth it. If you wait too long and they do it to you, then you end up force fielding vs the clusters and you can't cluster back immediately since it makes your defense harder (defending 1 more dmg per cluster you make). Wasn't relevant to do it in this game though.

I liked the way you played it. Zem timing looked good, blast into synth spam was good I think. Maybe turn 7 you're supposed to Xae + Conduit? Feels bad not synthing though, but 1 turn earlier on the Xae is pretty sweet, it makes 4 extra HP compared to DDC + Synth which is what you did, so it's 4 HP instead of DD + Synth in terms of the 1 turn of growth. So 2 gold + 2 green for 4 hp. Actually both lines look good.

Unfortunately a bit of a non-game cause master bot botched the opening. 😄

window.author: Spyrfyr
window.content:
gj! my main call outs would be:

1. you need a different build order if you are going for fab, you floated too much gold in the opener. I think as P1 if you want to rush fab you do 1.DD 2.CC 3.DD 4.Fab

But in this set I wouldn't try going breachproof. Mahar is not a breachproof unit anyway

For me the main units are Xeno Plasma Fab

I would buy Fab as an efficient first attacker/econ unit (it is a way to avoid ramming into absorb as early as you otherwise would) and then Xeno as best absorber and Plasma as best post-absorb threshold attack


2. You were buying Drones late into the game and then holding them next turn, also you bought an Animus in the lategame and made a Rhino with it the turn after. Not efficient. I'd say plan your defense for next turn if you can. Take your time, play slower time controls and practice counting in advance
